description: The familiar mineral substance sodium chloride is 'morisa', whether gathered from seawater, brine, or rock deposits. It seasons and preserves food and also enters many material processes. Chemistry uses salt for a wider class of compounds, so an exact chemical account keeps the particular substance visible.
articulatory_notes: The lips close for voiced nasal 'm' and open into rounded 'o'. A tap or brief trill releases stressed high-front 'i'; a narrow tongue groove then sends unvoiced 's' into final open 'a'.
usage_notes: Everyday salt needs no classifier. Source, preparation, or intended use can precede 'morisa'; a chemical or health account supplies its narrower guidance.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Salt once had to be read through brine, weather, storage, and the food it was meant to keep. Its ordinary white grains conceal a great deal of place-specific work.

semantic_domains.creation: a mineral substance used in food preservation and other material processes
semantic_domains.nature: sodium chloride occurring in seawater, brine, and mineral deposits
semantic_domains.physical: a crystalline ionic substance familiar as common salt
